entities.name: śabda
entities.cite: even these are vyakhyata and rupyate sabdena — explained, and *given their form*, through word

entities.name: svara
entities.cite: The notes of the scale — sadja and its fellows, the seven svaras — are pure audition, the least conceptual of objects

entities.name: sadja
entities.cite: the named scale is what carves it into sadja-versus-rsabha

entities.name: bheda
entities.cite: the notes are individuated as notes, held apart as a system of seven, only through their names and the theory that names them

entities.name: artha
entities.cite: all the varieties of objects, rest on sabda-matras, the measures of word

axioms.name: vyakhyate
axioms.cite: even these are vyakhyata and rupyate sabdena — explained, and *given their form*, through word

axioms.name: rupyate
axioms.cite: even these are vyakhyata and rupyate sabdena — explained, and *given their form*, through word

axioms.name: asraya
axioms.cite: all the varieties of objects, rest on sabda-matras, the measures of word

claims.name: vyakhyate
claims.cite: the differences beginning with the note sadja are explained and given form by word

claims.name: asraya
claims.cite: all the varieties of objects rest upon the measures of word

rejected_readings.label: intrinsic_distinction
rejected_readings.rendering: The musical notes have an intrinsic, pre-given distinction that word merely names and describes.
rejected_readings.why: Makes the distinction (bheda) prior to word, contradicting the verse's core claim that word GIVES FORM to the distinction. The commentary explicitly shows: 'the named scale is what carves it into sadja-versus-rsabha'—the carving requires word; there is no pre-existing division to discover.

rejected_readings.label: semantic_layer_only
rejected_readings.rendering: Only the meanings and conceptual categories of objects depend on word; the audible phenomena themselves exist independently.
rejected_readings.why: Restricts word-dependence to the cognitive layer, contradicting the verse's a fortiori argument. The commentary states: 'The untrained ear hears continuous pitch; the named scale is what carves it into sadja-versus-rsabha.' If even immediate auditory distinction (the hardest case) requires word for its individuation, then all objects certainly depend on word for their differentiation.

notes: The verse presents an a fortiori argument: if even the most immediate sensory distinction (the audible notes) is word-formed, then all varieties of objects must rest on word-measures. Entities map cleanly to the ontology; the core insight is that sabda constitutes (rupyate) the distinction of notes and thereby grounds (asraya) all object-varieties.
